group-by-multiple-columns.label: GROUP BY Multiple Columns
group-by-multiple-columns.summary: Define a more detailed report grain by grouping on a deliberate combination of dimensions.

sketch1.title: Choose Dimensions That Answer the Question

single-choice-1.title: Two-Column Grain
single-choice-1.prompt: What does one row represent when grouping by `region, category`?
single-choice-1.hint: Describe the row with both grouping dimensions.

help.concept: The group key is the combination of region and category.
help.hint_1: Either dimension can repeat in another combination.

single-choice-2.title: Effect of an Extra Grouping Column
single-choice-2.prompt: What happens if `product_name` is added to `GROUP BY region, category`?
single-choice-2.hint: Every additional grouping dimension creates a more detailed grain.

help.concept: The original groups are subdivided by product.
help.hint_1: The query remains aggregate and read-only.

options.b: The report splits into smaller region-category-product groups.

multi-choice-1.title: Valid Multi-Column Grains
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which report requests require two grouping columns? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Count the dimensions named in each request.

help.concept: Two “by” dimensions require a two-column group key.
help.hint_1: Units by category uses only one grouping field.

multi-choice-2.title: Multi-Column Grouping Practices
multi-choice-2.prompt: Which practices help keep the intended grain? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-2.hint: The selected dimensions should match the report contract exactly.

help.concept: Row identifiers usually destroy a summary grain.
help.hint_1: A written grain statement prevents accidental detail.
help.hint_2: Choose practices that preserve the requested combination.

options.a: State what one result row represents before writing SQL.
options.c: Add identifiers such as order_id even when the report should summarize.
options.d: Avoid dimensions not required by the question.

drag-reorder-1.title: Build a Two-Dimension Group
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the clauses for a region-category count in written SQL order.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Define the two dimensions, source, group key, then output order.

help.concept: GROUP BY follows FROM in written syntax.
help.hint_1: ORDER BY arranges the resulting combinations.

tokens.t1: SELECT region, category, COUNT(*) AS order_count

fill-blank-choice-1.title: Complete the Combination Grain
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: Fill the second grouping column for a region-category report.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Use the second dimension named in the report grain.

help.concept: The combination is region plus category.
help.hint_1: Measures are aggregated rather than used as the group key.

try_group_by_multiple_columns_sketch0.title: Gross Sales by Region and Category
try_group_by_multiple_columns_sketch0.prompt: Return `region`, `category`, and gross sales rounded to two decimals as `gross_sales`. Group by region and category, then sort by both dimensions.
try_group_by_multiple_columns_sketch0.hint: Both displayed dimensions must be included in GROUP BY.

help.concept: A multi-column GROUP BY creates one aggregate row for each distinct dimension combination.
help.hint_1: Keep region before category throughout the query.
help.hint_2: Aggregate gross sales within each pair.

try_group_by_multiple_columns_sketch0.solutionCode:
SELECT
    region,
    category,
    ROUND(SUM(quantity * unit_price), 2) AS gross_sales
FROM sales_reporting
GROUP BY region, category
ORDER BY region, category;

try_group_by_multiple_columns_sketch1.title: Orders by Representative and Status
try_group_by_multiple_columns_sketch1.prompt: Return `sales_rep`, `order_status`, and `COUNT(*)` as `order_count`. Group by representative and status, then sort by representative and status.
try_group_by_multiple_columns_sketch1.hint: Use only the two dimensions named in the reporting question.

help.concept: Every additional grouping column changes what one result row represents.
help.hint_1: Keep `sales_rep` and `order_status` in SELECT and GROUP BY.
help.hint_2: Do not add product or order identifiers.

try_group_by_multiple_columns_sketch1.solutionCode:
SELECT
    sales_rep,
    order_status,
    COUNT(*) AS order_count
FROM sales_reporting
GROUP BY sales_rep, order_status
ORDER BY sales_rep, order_status;

sketch0.title: Define a Combination Grain
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
Multiple grouping columns create one result row for each unique combination.

```sql
SELECT
    region,
    category,
    COUNT(*) AS order_count
FROM sales_reporting
GROUP BY region, category;
```

One row now represents one category within one region—not a region alone and not an individual order.

sketch1.title: Choose Dimensions That Answer the Question
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
A different pair of dimensions answers a different question.

```sql
SELECT
    sales_rep,
    order_status,
    COUNT(*) AS order_count
FROM sales_reporting
GROUP BY sales_rep, order_status;
```

This report shows workload status for each representative. Adding product name would split those rows further and change the question.
